Title: Identification and quantification of hydrocarbon functional groups in gasoline using 1H-NMR spectroscopy for property prediction
Abstract: Gasoline is one of the most important distillate fuels obtained from crude refining; it is mainly used as an automotive fuel to propel spark-ignited (SI) engines. It is a complex hydrocarbon fuel that is known to possess several hundred individual molecules of varying sizes and chemical classes. These large numbers of individual molecules can be assembled into a finite set of molecular moieties or functional groups that can independently represent the chemical composition. Identification and quantification of groups enables the prediction of many fuel properties that otherwise may be difficult and expensive to measure experimentally. In the present work, high resolution 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, an advanced structure elucidation technique, was employed for the molecular characterization of a gasoline sample in order to analyze the functional groups. The chemical composition of the gasoline sample was then expressed using six hydrocarbon functional groups, as follows: paraffinic groups (CH, CH2 and CH3), naphthenic CH-CH2 groups and aromatic C-CH groups. The obtained functional groups were then used to predict a number of fuel properties, including research octane number (RON), motor octane number (MON), derived cetane number (DCN), threshold sooting index (TSI) and yield sooting index (YSI).

Title: A simple NMR methodology for evaluating filter cake properties and drilling fluid-induced formation damage
Abstract: The filter cake formed during drilling is imperative for the success of the drilling operation. It also impacts the subsequent operations, such as cementing, completion, and production. However, the filter cake becomes detrimental if it cannot be easily removed or if its filtrate invades the formation and interacts with the formation fluids. To avoid formation damage, the drilling fluid should be designed such that the formed filter cake is thin and impermeable. The fluid loss volume and the filter cake thickness are routinely measured to quantify the severity of the filtration. However, the filter cake permeability, which is a critical property that controls the filtration process, is usually not measured due to the difficulty in measuring it. In this work, a simple NMR method was used to simultaneously estimate the filter cake thickness and permeability. We used four different drilling fluid formulations with different filtration characteristics to form filter cakes. We scanned the core samples before and after the filtration experiment and also at different times during the filtration process. From the NMR T2 response, we developed an approach to estimate the external filter cake thickness and the internal filter cake permeability. We validated our results by comparing the estimated filter cake thickness with physical measurement and the estimated filter cake permeability with the computed value using the filtration data. The validation results show good agreement with a maximum absolute percentage error of 8.33% in filter cake thickness determination and 4.95% in filter cake permeability determination. Our proposed method is quick, non-destructive, and eliminates the errors due to core heterogeneity, as each core serves as its own baseline. This study provides experimental evidence and analysis that would enable drilling engineers to evaluate filter cake properties and the associated formation damage in the laboratory in a simple way.

Title: A calibration method for estimating mudcake thickness and porosity using NMR data
Abstract: Drilling fluid invasion into petroleum reservoirs can cause formation damage and may render the reservoir unproductive if the drilling fluid design is not properly optimized. The process of drilling fluid invasion into the reservoir is partly controlled by the filter cake or mudcake deposited on the borehole wall during drilling. The mudcake properties (thickness and porosity) influence the rate of invasion. The control of invasion is important for successful drilling and logging operations. Current methods used for determining mudcake thickness and porosity are limited due to core disturbances or difficulty in making measurements under simulated downhole conditions. In this study, low field nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) was used to measure the thickness and porosity of mudcakes deposited by four different drilling fluid formulations. The accuracy of the NMR method was compared with physical measurement of thickness using a micrometer and porosity calculation from mass balance. We found that the NMR method is 90–96% accurate for thickness measurement and 88–94% accurate for porosity measurement, relative to the conventional methods. The NMR method enabled continuous, non-invasive monitoring of mudcake buildup in a non-destructive manner. This study provides a new NMR application that can be adapted for evaluating mudcake properties under realistic downhole conditions to provide useful information for drilling fluid design and formation damage control.

Title: Effects of Foam Microbubbles on Electrical Resistivity and Capillary Pressure of Partially Saturated Porous Media
Abstract: Laboratory measurements of capillary pressure (Pc) and the electrical resistivity index (RI) of reservoir rocks are used to calibrate well logging tools and to determine reservoir fluid distribution. Significant studies on the methods and factors affecting these measurements in rocks containing oil, gas, and water are adequately reported in the literature. However, with the advent of chemical enhanced oil recovery (EOR) methods, surfactants are mixed with injection fluids to generate foam to enhance the gas injection process. Foam is a complex and non-Newtonian fluid whose behavior in porous media is different from conventional reservoir fluids. As a result, the effect of foam on Pc and the reliability of using known rock models such as the Archie equation to fit experimental resistivity data in rocks containing foam are yet to be ascertained. In this study, we investigated the effect of foam on the behavior of both Pc and RI curves in sandstone and carbonate rocks using both porous plate and two-pole resistivity methods at ambient temperature. Our results consistently showed that for a given water saturation (Sw), the RI of a rock increases in the presence of foam than without foam. We found that, below a critical Sw, the resistivity of a rock containing foam continues to rise rapidly. We argue, based on knowledge of foam behavior in porous media, that this critical Sw represents the regime where the foam texture begins to become finer, and it is dependent on the properties of the rock and the foam. Nonetheless, the Archie model fits the experimental data of the rocks but with resulting saturation exponents that are higher than conventional gas–water rock systems. The degree of variation in the saturation exponents between the two fluid systems also depends on the rock and fluid properties. A theory is presented to explain this phenomenon. We also found that foam affects the saturation exponent in a similar way as oil-wet rocks in the sense that they decrease the cross-sectional area of water available in the pores for current flow. Foam appears to have competing and opposite effects caused by the presence of clay, micropores, and conducting minerals, which tend to lower the saturation exponent at low Sw. Finally, the Pc curve is consistently lower in foam than without foam for the same Sw.

Title: Advances in diffusivity measurement of solvents in oil sands
Water in oil emulsion droplet size characterization using a pulsed field gradient with diffusion editing (PFG-DE) NMR technique

Effect of mineral composition on transverse relaxation time distributions and MR imaging of tight rocks from offshore Ireland
Abstract: Tight reservoir rocks are characterized by low porosity and permeability, which complicates fluid flow and production. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is a widely used technique to assess pore space and fluid properties in such rocks. However, the interpretation of NMR data in tight rocks is challenging due to the complex pore structure and the presence of different minerals. In this study, we investigate the effect of mineral composition on transverse relaxation time (T2) distributions and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of tight rocks from offshore Ireland. We conducted NMR measurements on a suite of rock samples with varying mineralogy, including quartz, clay, and carbonate. The results show that the T2 distributions are significantly affected by the presence of paramagnetic minerals such as clays and iron-bearing carbonates, which enhance relaxation rates. MRI images reveal heterogeneous fluid distributions influenced by mineralogy and pore structure. This study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR data in tight rocks and highlights the importance of considering mineral composition when assessing reservoir quality.

Realistic molecular model of kerogen's nanostructure
Abstract: Despite kerogen's importance as the organic backbone for hydrocarbon production from source rocks such as gas shale, the interplay between kerogen's chemistry, morphology and mechanics remains unexplored. As the environmental impact of shale gas rises, identifying functional relations between its geochemical, transport, elastic and fracture properties from realistic molecular models of kerogens becomes all the more important. Here, by using a hybrid experimental–simulation method, we propose a panel of realistic molecular models of mature and immature kerogens that provide a detailed picture of kerogen's nanostructure without considering the presence of clays and other minerals in shales. We probe the models' strengths and limitations, and show that they predict essential features amenable to experimental validation, including pore distribution, vibrational density of states and stiffness. We also show that kerogen's maturation, which manifests itself as an increase in the sp2/sp3 hybridization ratio, entails a crossover from plastic-to-brittle rupture mechanisms.

Title: Determination of porosity types from NMR data and their relationship to porosity types derived from thin section
Using MRI to study in situ oil recovery during CO2 injection in carbonates
A whole-body fast field-cycling scanner for clinical molecular imaging studies
Abstract: Fast Field-Cycling (FFC) MRI is an emerging technique that allows the exploration of novel contrast mechanisms dependent on the magnetic field strength. These can highlight, for example, the concentration of specific biomarkers or the molecular dynamics in tissues. While FFC has been demonstrated in small animals, translation to humans is very challenging because the magnetic fields must be switched rapidly over a large volume. Here we present the first whole-body FFC-MRI scanner designed for human studies. The system uses a novel magnet design that produces a magnetic field which can be switched rapidly between 0 and 200 mT, with a uniformity of better than 20 ppm over a 30 cm diameter sphere. We demonstrate that high quality anatomical images can be obtained with different field strengths and that dispersion images can be generated, highlighting the potential of the method for clinical research.

Title: Measurements of fractional wettability of oil fields' rocks by the nuclear magnetic relaxation method
Importance of classical diffusion in NMR studies of water in biological cells
Abstract: The nuclear-magnetic-resonance behavior of water in heterogeneous systems, particularly biological cells, is often strongly influenced by the presence of paramagnetic ions and macromolecules which are distributed nonuniformly throughout the sample. The purpose of this paper is to demonstrate theoretically that, for a wide class of heterogeneous systems, the qualitative features of the observed signal are determined by the rate of diffusion of the nuclear spins between regions of different relaxation characteristic. The experimentally observed relaxation times depend on the size of the regions, the diffusion coefficient, and the surface relaxation strength. The theory shows that measurements of the magnetic-field dependence of the relaxation times can be used to obtain estimates of cellular dimensions and permeabilities.

Title: Oil and water content measurements in bitumen ore and froth samples using low field NMR
Abstract: Low-field NMR (nuclear magnetic resonance) has been used to measure oil and water content in bitumen ore and froth samples. The NMR technique is non-destructive and requires little sample preparation. The oil and water contents are determined from the NMR signal amplitudes, which are proportional to the hydrogen index of each phase. The hydrogen index of bitumen is less than that of water, and it varies with temperature and bitumen type. A calibration procedure is presented to account for this variation. The NMR results are compared with those from Dean-Stark extraction, showing good agreement. The NMR method can be used for process control in oil sands extraction plants.

Title: In-situ viscosity of heavy oil: core and log calibrations
Petrophysical characterization of Chinese coal cores with heat treatment by nuclear magnetic resonance
Abstract: Heat treatment is an important method for enhancing coalbed methane recovery. In this study, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) was used to investigate the petrophysical properties of Chinese coal cores before and after heat treatment. The results show that heat treatment causes significant changes in pore structure, porosity, and permeability. The NMR T2 spectra reveal that heat treatment increases the pore volume, especially in the macropore range. The porosity and permeability increase with increasing temperature up to 600 °C, but decrease at higher temperatures due to pore collapse. The NMR-derived permeability correlates well with the measured permeability. This study demonstrates that NMR is an effective tool for characterizing the petrophysical properties of coal cores during heat treatment.

Title: Translational Dynamics and Magnetic Resonance
Principles of nuclear magnetic resonance microscopy
Effects of diffusion on free precession in nuclear magnetic resonance experiments
Abstract: The effect of diffusion on free precession in nuclear magnetic resonance experiments is analyzed. It is shown that diffusion in an inhomogeneous magnetic field leads to an additional decay of the spin echo amplitude. The decay constant depends on the diffusion coefficient, the magnetic field gradient, and the time between pulses. A method is presented for measuring diffusion coefficients using the spin echo technique. Experimental results for water and glycerol are given, showing good agreement with theory.

Title: Measurement of rock-core capillary pressure curves using a single-speed centrifuge and one-dimensional magnetic-resonance imaging
Abstract: A new method for measuring capillary pressure curves in rock cores is presented. The method combines a single-speed centrifuge with one-dimensional magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to measure the fluid saturation distribution along the core. From the saturation distribution, the capillary pressure curve can be derived. The method is faster than traditional multi-speed centrifuge methods and provides continuous saturation profiles. Results for sandstone and carbonate cores are presented and compared with conventional methods, showing good agreement.

Title: NMR wettability indices: effect of OBM on wettability and NMR responses
Abstract: Wettability is a key factor controlling fluid distribution and flow in petroleum reservoirs. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) has been used to derive wettability indices based on the relaxation time of fluids in porous media. This paper investigates the effect of oil-based mud (OBM) filtrate on wettability and NMR responses. Core flooding experiments were performed with OBM and brine, and NMR measurements were made at different saturation states. The results show that OBM can alter the wettability of the rock, making it more oil-wet. This change is reflected in the NMR relaxation times and the derived wettability indices. The study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR logs in wells drilled with OBM.

Systematic optimization approach for high-resolution NMR logging
Determination of permeability from NMR T1/T2 ratio in carbonates
A new characterization of bulk-volume irreducible using magnetic resonance
The magnetic resonance imaging log characterized by comparison with petrophysical properties and laboratory core data
New large-hole magnetic resonance logging-while-drilling tool with short inter-echo time and improved vertical resolution
Lateral-motion correction of NMR logging-while-drilling data
Capillary trapping quantification in sandstones using NMR relaxometry
Abstract: Capillary trapping is an important mechanism for geological storage of CO2. This study uses nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) relaxometry to quantify capillary trapping in sandstones. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements were performed on sandstone cores during drainage and imbibition cycles with supercritical CO2 and brine. The results show that the amount of trapped CO2 can be quantified from the NMR signal. The trapped saturation depends on the initial CO2 saturation and the pore structure. The NMR method provides a non-destructive way to measure capillary trapping in porous media.

Title: Simulation and experimental measurements of internal magnetic field gradients and NMR transverse relaxation times (T2) in sandstone rocks
Abstract: Internal magnetic field gradients in porous media arise from susceptibility differences between the solid matrix and the pore fluid. These gradients affect NMR transverse relaxation times (T2) and can complicate the interpretation of NMR data. This study combines numerical simulations and experimental measurements to investigate internal gradients in sandstone rocks. The simulations use realistic pore geometries from micro-CT images. The results show that internal gradients are highly heterogeneous and depend on the pore structure and the magnetic susceptibility of the minerals. The simulations are validated by comparison with experimental T2 measurements at different magnetic field strengths. The study provides insights into the effect of internal gradients on NMR relaxation in sandstones.

Title: Pulsed field gradient stimulated echo methods for improved NMR diffusion measurements in heterogeneous systems
Probing interactions by means of pulsed field gradient nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy
Development of organic porosity in the Woodford Shale with increasing thermal maturity
Abstract: Organic porosity in shales is important for gas storage and flow. This study investigates the development of organic porosity in the Woodford Shale with increasing thermal maturity. Samples with vitrinite reflectance ranging from 0.5% to 9% were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and image analysis. The results show that organic porosity increases with thermal maturity up to a reflectance of about 3%, then decreases at higher maturities due to carbonization. The pores are mainly within the organic matter and range in size from a few nanometers to hundreds of nanometers. The development of organic porosity is related to the generation and expulsion of hydrocarbons.

Title: Effect of paramagnetic species on T 1, T 2 and T 1 /T 2 NMR relaxation times of liquids in porous CuSO 4 /Al 2 O 3
Interpretation of nuclear-magnetic-resonance response to hydrocarbons: application to miscible enhanced-oil-recovery experiments in shales
Abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is used to characterize hydrocarbons in shales. This study applies NMR to monitor miscible enhanced oil recovery (EOR) experiments in shale cores. The cores were saturated with oil and then flooded with a miscible solvent. NMR T1-T2 maps were acquired at different stages to track the oil and solvent. The results show that the NMR response changes as the oil is displaced and mixed with the solvent. The T1/T2 ratio can be used to distinguish between oil and solvent. The NMR method provides insights into the EOR process at the pore scale.

Title: Impact of saltwater environments on the coalescence of oil-in-water emulsions stabilized by an anionic surfactant
Abstract: Oil-in-water emulsions are used in various industrial applications, including oil recovery and environmental remediation. This study investigates the impact of saltwater on the coalescence of emulsions stabilized by an anionic surfactant. The emulsion stability was characterized by droplet size measurements, interfacial rheology, and coalescence time experiments. The results show that increasing salt concentration reduces the electrostatic repulsion between droplets, leading to faster coalescence. The effect depends on the type and concentration of salt. The study provides insights into the behavior of emulsions in saline environments.

Title: Compact magnets for magnetic resonance
A next-generation wireline NMR logging tool
Principles in NMR spectroscopy
Effect of salt on the inter-relationship between the morphological, emulsifying and interfacial rheological properties of O/W emulsions at oil/water interface
Abstract: This study investigates the effect of salt on the properties of oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions. The morphological, emulsifying, and interfacial rheological properties were characterized at different salt concentrations. The results show that salt affects the droplet size distribution, emulsion stability, and interfacial film properties. The interfacial rheology reveals that salt strengthens the interfacial film at low concentrations but weakens it at high concentrations. The study provides insights into the role of salt in emulsion stability.

Title: NMR characterization of a tight sand's pore structures and fluid mobility: an experimental investigation for CO2 EOR potential
Abstract: Tight sand reservoirs have complex pore structures and low permeability, making oil recovery challenging. This study uses nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) to characterize the pore structures and fluid mobility of tight sand cores. NMR T2 spectra were measured on cores saturated with brine and oil. The results show that the pore size distribution is multimodal, with micropores, mesopores, and macropores. Fluid mobility is controlled by the pore size and connectivity. CO2 flooding experiments were performed to evaluate enhanced oil recovery (EOR) potential. The NMR results show that CO2 can recover oil from mesopores and macropores, but not from micropores. The study provides insights into CO2 EOR in tight sands.

Title: Multinuclear NMR microscopy of two-phase fluid systems in porous rock
Well logging for earth scientists
The effect of clay content on the spin-spin NMR relaxation time measured in porous media
Abstract: Clay minerals in porous media affect NMR relaxation times and can complicate the interpretation of NMR logs. This study investigates the effect of clay content on the spin-spin (T2) relaxation time in sandstones. Artificial sandstones with different clay types and contents were prepared and saturated with brine. NMR T2 measurements were performed, and the results were compared with those from clean sandstones. The results show that clay reduces the T2 relaxation time due to surface relaxation and the presence of microporosity. The effect depends on the clay type and distribution. The study provides a basis for correcting NMR logs for clay content.

Title: A new method to evaluate reaction kinetics of acids with carbonate rocks using NMR diffusion measurements
Abstract: Acid stimulation is used to enhance productivity in carbonate reservoirs. This study presents a new method to evaluate the reaction kinetics of acids with carbonate rocks using NMR diffusion measurements. The method uses pulsed field gradient (PFG) NMR to measure the diffusion coefficient of acid in the rock during the reaction. The change in diffusion coefficient reflects the consumption of acid and the generation of reaction products. The reaction rate constant can be derived from the diffusion data. The method is applied to different acids and carbonate rocks, showing good agreement with conventional methods. The NMR method is non-destructive and provides real-time monitoring of the reaction.

Title: An experimental study on the effect of magnetic field strength and internal gradient on NMR-Derived petrophysical properties of sandstones
Abstract: NMR-derived petrophysical properties such as porosity, permeability, and pore size distribution depend on the magnetic field strength and internal gradients. This study investigates the effect of field strength and internal gradients on NMR measurements in sandstones. NMR T2 measurements were performed on sandstone cores at different field strengths (2 MHz and 12 MHz). The results show that porosity is independent of field strength, but T2 relaxation times and derived pore size distributions are affected. Internal gradients increase with field strength and cause additional relaxation. The effect is more pronounced in rocks with paramagnetic minerals. The study provides guidelines for selecting the appropriate field strength for NMR core analysis.

Title: New technique for evaluating fracture geometry and preferential orientation using pulsed field gradient nuclear magnetic resonance
NMR imaging for water/oil displacement in cores under viscous-capillary force control
Construction of synthetic capillary pressure curves from the joint use of NMR log data and conventional well logs
Abstract: Capillary pressure curves are important for reservoir characterization but are not directly available from logs. This study presents a method to construct synthetic capillary pressure curves from the joint use of NMR log data and conventional well logs. The method uses NMR T2 distributions to derive pore size distributions and combines them with porosity and permeability from conventional logs. The capillary pressure curves are constructed using a pore-scale model. The method is applied to a carbonate reservoir and validated with core data, showing good agreement. The synthetic curves can be used for reservoir modeling and simulation.

Title: Source rock characterization method for petroleum exploration
Ultra-Fast Capillary Pressure and Resistivity measurements
NMR diffusion editing for D – T 2 maps: application to recognition of wettability change
Quantitative evaluation of porous media wettability using NMR relaxometry
Characterization of shales using T1–T2 NMR maps
Effect of paramagnetic ions on NMR relaxation of fluids at solid surfaces

Fluid characterization using nuclear magnetic resonance logging
A new NMR method of fluid characterization in reservoir rocks: experimental confirmation and simulation results
Abstract: A new nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) method for fluid characterization in reservoir rocks is presented. The method uses a combination of NMR relaxation and diffusion measurements to identify and quantify different fluids (water, oil, and gas) in the pore space. The experimental confirmation was performed on core samples partially saturated with crude oil and brine. The results show that the method can distinguish between oil and water and provide estimates of fluid saturations. Simulation results demonstrate the sensitivity of the method to fluid properties and pore structure. The new method has potential applications in NMR logging for reservoir evaluation.

Title: Wettability, saturation, and viscosity from NMR measurements
Abstract: This paper presents a method for estimating wettability, fluid saturations, and viscosity from nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) measurements in reservoir rocks. The method is based on analysis of NMR relaxation time distributions and diffusion measurements. Wettability is inferred from the relaxation times of oil and water, which are affected by surface interactions. Saturations are determined from the amplitudes of the NMR signals. Viscosity is estimated from the relaxation times of oil, which depend on molecular dynamics. The method was tested on core samples with different wettability and fluid properties. The results show good agreement with independent measurements.

Title: Influence of mud filtrate on the pore system of different sandstone rocks
Abstract: Mud filtrate invasion during drilling can alter the pore system of reservoir rocks and affect petrophysical evaluations. This study investigates the influence of mud filtrate on the pore system of different sandstone rocks using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and other techniques. Core samples were exposed to mud filtrate under simulated downhole conditions. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements were performed before and after exposure to assess changes in pore size distribution and porosity. The results show that mud filtrate can cause fines migration, pore blocking, and changes in pore connectivity. The effect depends on the rock mineralogy and the mud filtrate composition. The study provides insights into formation damage mechanisms and the interpretation of NMR logs in wells drilled with mud.

Title: Determining the transverse surface relaxivity of reservoir rocks: a critical review and perspective
Abstract: Surface relaxivity is a key parameter for converting NMR relaxation time distributions to pore size distributions. This paper provides a critical review of methods for determining the transverse surface relaxivity of reservoir rocks. The review covers experimental methods, including NMR on saturated and partially saturated rocks, and theoretical models. The factors affecting surface relaxivity, such as mineralogy, surface roughness, and fluid properties, are discussed. The paper also presents a perspective on future research directions, including the use of advanced imaging techniques and multi-scale modeling. The review aims to provide guidance for selecting appropriate surface relaxivity values in NMR petrophysics.

Title: An investigation into the effects of pore connectivity on T NMR relaxation
Abstract: Pore connectivity affects fluid flow and NMR relaxation in porous media. This study investigates the effects of pore connectivity on NMR T2 relaxation using numerical simulations and experimental measurements. Pore networks with different connectivity were generated, and NMR relaxation was simulated. The results show that pore connectivity affects the relaxation time distribution, with higher connectivity leading to faster relaxation due to enhanced diffusion coupling between pores. Experimental measurements on rock cores with different pore structures confirm the simulation results. The study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR data in terms of pore connectivity.

Title: Measuring adsorption, diffusion and flow in chemical engineering: applications of magnetic resonance to porous media
Abstract: Magnetic resonance (MR) techniques provide non-invasive measurements of adsorption, diffusion, and flow in porous media. This review covers the principles and applications of MR in chemical engineering. The techniques discussed include NMR relaxometry, diffusometry, and imaging (MRI). Applications include catalyst characterization, reactor engineering, and fluid transport in reservoir rocks. The review highlights the ability of MR to probe processes at the pore scale and to provide quantitative data for model validation. Future developments in MR technology and data analysis are also discussed.

Title: Deriving capillary pressure and water saturation from NMR transversal relaxation times
Surface nuclear magnetic relaxation and dynamics of water and oil in macroporous media
Abstract: Surface nuclear magnetic relaxation of water and oil in macroporous media is studied theoretically and experimentally. A model is developed that accounts for the dynamics of fluids near the pore surface, including surface diffusion and relaxation. The model predicts the dependence of relaxation times on pore size, surface relaxivity, and fluid properties. Experimental measurements on model porous media and rock cores confirm the model predictions. The study provides insights into the mechanisms of surface relaxation and the interpretation of NMR data in terms of pore structure and fluid dynamics.

Title: Characterization and modeling of a thick carbonate transition zone
Experimental evaluation on the oil saturation and movability in the organic and inorganic matter of shale
Abstract: Oil saturation and movability in shale are critical for evaluating shale oil resources. This study uses nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) to evaluate oil saturation and movability in the organic and inorganic matter of shale. Core samples from a shale oil reservoir were analyzed using NMR T1-T2 maps. The results show that oil in organic matter has shorter relaxation times than oil in inorganic matter, allowing them to be distinguished. The movable oil fraction is higher in inorganic matter than in organic matter. The study provides a method for quantifying oil distribution and movability in shale, which can be used for resource assessment and production planning.

Title: Spin-spin relaxation time investigation of oil/brine/sand systems. Kinetics, effects of salinity, and implications for wettability and bitumen recovery
Abstract: The spin-spin (T2) relaxation time of oil/brine/sand systems is investigated to understand the effects of salinity on wettability and bitumen recovery. NMR T2 measurements were performed on sand packs with different brine salinities and oil types. The results show that T2 relaxation times are sensitive to the distribution of oil and brine in the pore space, which is controlled by wettability. Increasing salinity reduces the electrostatic repulsion between the oil and sand, making the system more oil-wet. This affects the T2 relaxation times and the efficiency of bitumen recovery. The study provides insights into the role of brine chemistry in wettability and oil recovery.

Title: Heavy oil fluid testing with conventional and novel techniques
Topical magnetic resonance
An improved technique for deriving drainage capillary pressure from NMR T 2 distributions
Oil/water imbibition and drainage capillary pressure determined by MRI on a wide sampling of rocks
A laboratory study of NMR relaxation times and pore coupling in heterogeneous media
Abstract: NMR relaxation times in heterogeneous media are affected by pore coupling due to diffusion. This laboratory study investigates the effects of pore coupling on NMR T2 relaxation times in unconsolidated sediments with different pore structures. The results show that pore coupling can cause the relaxation time distribution to deviate from the pore size distribution, especially when the pores are well connected. The degree of coupling depends on the diffusion coefficient, pore size, and surface relaxivity. The study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR data in heterogeneous media and the conditions under which pore coupling is important.

Title: A laboratory study of NMR relaxation times in unconsolidated heterogeneous sediments
Abstract: This laboratory study investigates NMR relaxation times in unconsolidated heterogeneous sediments. The sediments have different grain size distributions and mineralogies. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements were performed on water-saturated samples. The results show that the T2 distribution reflects the pore size distribution, but is also affected by the presence of paramagnetic minerals and the surface relaxivity. The study provides a database of NMR relaxation times for different sediment types and discusses the implications for NMR logging in unconsolidated aquifers.

Title: Wettability effects in a sandstone reservoir and outcrop cores from NMR relaxation time distributions
Abstract: Wettability effects on NMR relaxation time distributions are investigated in sandstone reservoir and outcrop cores. The cores were saturated with brine and crude oil, and NMR T2 measurements were performed at different saturation states. The results show that the T2 distribution is sensitive to wettability, with oil-wet pores having different relaxation characteristics than water-wet pores. The changes in the T2 distribution with saturation can be used to infer wettability. The study compares the NMR responses of reservoir and outcrop cores and discusses the implications for wettability evaluation from NMR logs.

Title: Unsaturated water flow within porous materials observed by NMR imaging
Abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) imaging is used to observe unsaturated water flow within porous materials. The technique provides non-invasive, quantitative measurements of water content distribution as a function of time. Experiments were performed on plaster and sandstone samples during imbibition and drying. The results show the evolution of water content profiles and demonstrate the ability of NMR imaging to study flow processes in porous media. The technique has applications in hydrology, soil science, and civil engineering.

Title: Nuclear magnetic resonance T1–T2 spectra in heavy oil reservoirs
Abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) T1-T2 spectra are used to characterize heavy oil reservoirs. The T1-T2 maps provide information on fluid types and pore sizes. In heavy oil reservoirs, the NMR response is complicated by the high viscosity and complex composition of the oil. This study presents T1-T2 measurements on core samples from heavy oil reservoirs. The results show that heavy oil has distinct T1-T2 signatures that can be used to distinguish it from water and light oil. The T1/T2 ratio is correlated with oil viscosity. The study demonstrates the potential of T1-T2 maps for evaluating heavy oil reservoirs.

Title: Advances in low-field nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) technologies applied for characterization of pore space inside rocks: a critical review
Abstract: Low-field nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) has become a widely used technique for characterizing pore space in rocks. This paper provides a critical review of advances in low-field NMR technologies applied to pore space characterization. The review covers the principles of NMR relaxation and diffusion, experimental methods, and data analysis techniques. Applications include porosity, pore size distribution, permeability, and fluid typing. The paper also discusses the challenges and limitations of low-field NMR, such as the effects of internal gradients and paramagnetic minerals. Future developments in hardware and software are outlined.

Title: Insight into oil and gas-shales compounds signatures in low field 1H NMR and its application in porosity evaluation
Abstract: Low-field 1H NMR is used to study oil and gas shales. The NMR signals from shales come from both the organic matter and the pore fluids. This study investigates the signatures of organic compounds and pore fluids in low-field NMR and their application in porosity evaluation. The results show that the NMR T2 distribution can be decomposed into components corresponding to organic matter, clay-bound water, and movable fluids. The porosity derived from NMR agrees with conventional measurements. The study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR data in shales and the quantification of porosity.

Title: Nuclear induction due to free larmor precession
Abstract: This paper reports the observation of nuclear induction signals due to free Larmor precession. The signals are detected after a radiofrequency pulse and decay with a time constant T2. The experiment demonstrates the principle of spin echo formation. The results are in agreement with the Bloch equations. This work laid the foundation for pulsed NMR spectroscopy.

Title: Spin echoes
Abstract: The spin echo phenomenon is described theoretically and experimentally. A sequence of two radiofrequency pulses produces an echo signal at a time equal to the pulse spacing. The echo amplitude is affected by diffusion in an inhomogeneous magnetic field. The spin echo method provides a way to measure relaxation times and diffusion coefficients. This paper is a classic in the field of NMR.

Title: Performance analysis of thermochemical fluids in removing the gas condensate from different gas formations
New generation magnetic resonance while drilling
Design and implementation of a new magnetic resonance tool for the while drilling environment
The impact of pore type on NMR T2 and micp in bioclastic carbonate reservoirs
Study of vuggy carbonates using NMR and X-ray CT scanning
Abstract: Vuggy carbonates have complex pore structures that are difficult to characterize. This study combines nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and X-ray computed tomography (CT) scanning to study vuggy carbonates. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements provide information on pore size distribution and fluid saturation. X-ray CT provides 3D images of the pore structure, including the distribution of vugs. The combined data are used to understand the relationship between pore structure and NMR response. The study shows that vugs have a characteristic NMR signature and that the NMR-derived pore size distribution can be correlated with the CT-derived vug size distribution.

Title: Quantification of microporosity by nuclear magnetic resonance relaxation of water imbibed in porous media
Abstract: Microporosity in porous media affects fluid storage and transport. This study presents a method for quantifying microporosity using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) relaxation of water imbibed in the pores. The method is based on the fact that water in micropores has shorter relaxation times than water in macropores due to surface relaxation. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements were performed on porous media with different microporosities. The results show that the amplitude of the short T2 component is proportional to the microporosity. The method is applied to soil and rock samples and validated with independent measurements.

Title: Measurement of emulsion droplet sizes using PFG NMR and regularization methods
Abstract: Pulsed field gradient (PFG) NMR is used to measure emulsion droplet sizes. The method is based on the restricted diffusion of molecules within droplets. The NMR signal attenuation as a function of gradient strength is analyzed using regularization methods to obtain the droplet size distribution. The method is applied to oil-in-water emulsions with different droplet sizes. The results are compared with optical microscopy and show good agreement. The PFG NMR method is non-invasive and can be used to monitor emulsion stability over time.

Title: Applications of a new magnetic resonance logging-while-drilling tool in a gulf of mexico deepwater development project
NMR derived capillary pressure and relative permeability curves as an aid in rock typing of carbonate reservoirs
The signal-to-noise ratio of the nuclear magnetic resonance experiment
Quantitative estimates of porous media wettability from proton NMR measurements
Determination of pore size distribution in sedimentary rocks by proton nuclear magnetic resonance
Abstract: Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is used to determine pore size distributions in sedimentary rocks. The method is based on the relationship between the T2 relaxation time and the pore size. NMR T2 measurements were performed on water-saturated sandstone and carbonate cores. The T2 distributions were converted to pore size distributions using a surface relaxivity calibrated from mercury injection capillary pressure (MICP) data. The NMR-derived pore size distributions agree with MICP data. The study demonstrates that NMR can provide quantitative pore size information for reservoir rocks.

Title: Proton magnetic resonance and pore size variations in reservoir sandstones
Abstract: Proton magnetic resonance (NMR) is used to study pore size variations in reservoir sandstones. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements were performed on a suite of sandstone samples with different pore sizes. The results show that the T2 distribution reflects the pore size distribution, with larger pores having longer T2 times. The relationship between T2 and pore size is affected by surface relaxivity, which varies with mineralogy. The study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR logs in terms of pore size and reservoir quality.

Title: Nmr In partially saturated rocks: laboratory insights on free fluid index and comparison with borehole logs
Principles and limitations of NMR diffusion measurements
Effective gradients in porous media due to susceptibility differences
Effective gradients in porous media due to susceptibility differences
Abstract: Internal magnetic field gradients in porous media arise from susceptibility differences between the solid matrix and the pore fluid. These gradients affect NMR relaxation and diffusion measurements. This paper presents a theoretical analysis of the effective gradients in porous media. The analysis shows that the gradients are heterogeneous and depend on the pore structure and the susceptibility contrast. The results are compared with experimental measurements on rock cores. The study provides insights into the effects of internal gradients on NMR data and methods to mitigate them.

Title: NMR well logging
NMR logs help formation testing and evaluation
Evaluation and development of complex clastic reservoirs using NMR
Drainage mechanisms in gas reservoirs with bimodal pores – a core and pore scale study
Abstract: Gas reservoirs with bimodal pores have complex drainage mechanisms. This study investigates drainage mechanisms in such reservoirs using core and pore scale experiments. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) was used to monitor fluid saturation during drainage. The results show that gas first invades larger pores, then smaller pores. The capillary pressure curve is bimodal, reflecting the two pore sizes. The NMR T2 distribution changes during drainage, providing insights into the pore-scale fluid distribution. The study helps to understand gas recovery from bimodal pore reservoirs.

Title: Characterization of fluid drainage mechanism at core and pore scales: an NMR capillary pressure–based saturation exponent prediction
Motion artifact free data delivery in real time from a low gradient nmr tool
Remote (inside-out) NMR. III. Detection of nuclear magnetic resonance in a remotely produced region of homogeneous magnetic field
Abstract: This paper describes the detection of nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) in a region of homogeneous magnetic field produced remotely from the magnet. The technique, known as inside-out NMR, uses a permanent magnet designed to create a homogeneous field spot outside the tool. NMR signals are detected from fluids in that spot. The method has applications in well logging and materials testing. The paper presents the theoretical basis and experimental demonstration of the technique.

Title: Evaluation of 1H NMR relaxometry for the assessment of pore-size distribution in soil samples
Abstract: 1H NMR relaxometry is evaluated for assessing pore-size distribution in soil samples. The method is based on the relationship between the T2 relaxation time and the pore size. NMR T2 measurements were performed on water-saturated soil samples with different textures. The T2 distributions were converted to pore-size distributions using a surface relaxivity calibrated from mercury intrusion porosimetry. The NMR-derived pore-size distributions agree with porosimetry data for larger pores but underestimate microporosity. The study discusses the potential and limitations of NMR relaxometry for soil characterization.

Title: Study on the effects of heterogeneous distribution of methane hydrate on permeability of porous media using low-field NMR technique
Abstract: The heterogeneous distribution of methane hydrate in porous media affects permeability. This study uses low-field NMR to investigate the effects of hydrate distribution on permeability. Methane hydrate was formed in sand packs under different conditions, resulting in different hydrate distributions. NMR T2 measurements were used to monitor hydrate formation and to characterize the pore space. Permeability was measured before and after hydrate formation. The results show that permeability reduction depends on the hydrate distribution, with pore-filling hydrates causing less reduction than grain-cementing hydrates. The study provides insights into the permeability of hydrate-bearing sediments.

Title: NMR studies of emulsions
Abstract: This review covers NMR studies of emulsions. NMR techniques, including relaxometry, diffusometry, and imaging, are used to characterize emulsion properties such as droplet size, distribution, and stability. The principles of each technique are explained, and applications to oilfield and food emulsions are discussed. NMR provides non-invasive, quantitative information that is complementary to other methods. The review also discusses challenges and future directions in NMR emulsion science.

Title: Sizing of emulsion droplets under flow using flow-compensating NMR-PFG techniques
Mobile NMR and MRI, new developments in NMR
Diffusion ordered nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy: principles and applications
Analytic theory of two-dimensional NMR in systems with coupled macro- and micropores
Abstract: An analytic theory of two-dimensional NMR (T2-T2 exchange) in systems with coupled macro- and micropores is presented. The theory accounts for diffusion coupling between pores of different sizes. The 2D NMR correlation spectrum shows cross-peaks that indicate exchange between pores. The theory predicts the dependence of the cross-peaks on pore sizes, coupling strength, and experimental parameters. The results are relevant for interpreting 2D NMR data in rocks and other porous media with hierarchical pore structures.

Title: Real-time integrated petrophysics: geosteering in challenging geology and fluid systems
Characterizing pore-scale structure-flow correlations in sedimentary rocks using magnetic resonance imaging
Abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is used to characterize pore-scale structure-flow correlations in sedimentary rocks. The study combines MRI measurements of pore structure and fluid flow in the same rock sample. The pore structure is characterized using 3D MRI images, and fluid flow is characterized using velocity imaging. The results show correlations between pore size, connectivity, and flow velocity. The study provides insights into the relationship between pore structure and transport properties in rocks.

Title: Low-field NMR spectrometry of chalk and argillaceous sandstones: rock-fluid affinity assessed from T1/T2 ratio
NMR relaxometry in shale and implications for logging
High- and low-field NMR relaxometry and diffusometry of the bakken petroleum system
A laboratory study to determine the effect of iron oxides on proton NMR measurements
Abstract: Iron oxides in rocks and sediments affect proton NMR measurements by enhancing surface relaxation. This laboratory study investigates the effect of iron oxides on NMR T2 relaxation times. Synthetic samples with controlled iron oxide content and distribution were prepared. NMR measurements show that iron oxides reduce T2 relaxation times and can mask the pore size information. The effect depends on the iron oxide concentration and its proximity to the pore surface. The study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR data in iron-rich formations.

Title: Nuclear magnetic resonance relaxation measurements as a means of monitoring iron mineralization processes
Abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) relaxation measurements are used to monitor iron mineralization processes in porous media. The precipitation of iron oxides changes the surface relaxivity and affects the NMR signal. Experiments were performed on sand columns during iron oxide precipitation. NMR T2 measurements were acquired over time and correlated with the amount of iron precipitated. The results show that NMR can detect the onset and progress of iron mineralization. The method has potential applications in environmental monitoring and geobiology.

Title: Petrophysical principles of applications of NMR logging
A three-part study of NMR longitudinal relaxation properties of water-saturated sandstones
Abstract: This three-part study investigates the NMR longitudinal relaxation (T1) properties of water-saturated sandstones. Part 1 presents experimental T1 measurements on a variety of sandstones. Part 2 develops a model relating T1 to pore size distribution and surface relaxivity. Part 3 discusses the implications for permeability estimation from NMR. The study shows that T1 is sensitive to pore size and that the T1 distribution can be used to estimate permeability. The results provide a foundation for NMR logging applications.

Title: An introduction to the history of NMR well logging
Mechanism of NMR relaxation of fluids in rock
Abstract: The mechanism of NMR relaxation of fluids in rock is investigated theoretically and experimentally. The relaxation is controlled by surface interactions and diffusion. A model is developed that accounts for the effects of pore size, surface relaxivity, and diffusion coupling. The model predicts the dependence of T1 and T2 on these parameters. Experimental measurements on rock cores confirm the model. The study provides a fundamental understanding of NMR relaxation in rocks, which is essential for interpreting NMR logs.

Title: Borehole measurement of NMR characteristics of earth formations, and interpretations thereof
Nuclear magnetic resonance of rocks: T1 vs T2
NMR logging to estimate hydraulic conductivity in unconsolidated aquifers
Confinement effects on dipolar relaxation by translational dynamics of liquids in porous silica glasses
Abstract: The effect of confinement on the nuclear magnetic relaxation of liquids in porous silica glasses is investigated theoretically and experimentally. A model is developed that accounts for the translational dynamics of liquid molecules near the pore surface and their effect on dipolar relaxation. The model predicts that the relaxation rate is enhanced in confined geometries due to the reduced dimensionality of molecular motion. Experimental measurements on liquids in porous silica glasses with different pore sizes confirm the model predictions. The study provides insights into the molecular dynamics of confined liquids and the interpretation of NMR relaxation data in porous media.

Title: Slimhole application of magnetic resonance while drilling
Close monitoring of gel based conformance control by NMR techniques
Pros and cons of ultra-high-field MRI/MRS for human application
Abstract: Ultra-high-field (UHF) magnetic resonance imaging and spectroscopy (MRI/MRS) at field strengths of 7 Tesla and above offer significant advantages over lower fields, including increased signal-to-noise ratio, contrast, and spectral resolution. However, UHF also presents challenges such as increased field inhomogeneity, specific absorption rate, and safety concerns. This review discusses the pros and cons of UHF MRI/MRS for human applications. Topics covered include hardware developments, RF coil design, imaging techniques, and clinical applications. The review also addresses the safety and regulatory aspects of UHF. The aim is to provide a balanced perspective on the current state and future potential of UHF MRI/MRS.

Title: Variation of limestone pore structure under acidizing and wormhole propagation visualization using NMR
A review of alternative methods to classify rock-types from capillary pressure measurements
A novel NMR surface relaxivity measurements on rock cuttings for conventional and unconventional reservoirs
Abstract: Surface relaxivity is a key parameter for converting NMR relaxation time distributions to pore size distributions. This study presents a novel method for measuring surface relaxivity on rock cuttings, which are readily available from drilling operations. The method uses NMR T2 measurements on cuttings saturated with different fluids. The surface relaxivity is derived from the relationship between T2 and the surface-to-volume ratio, which is estimated from the cutting size and porosity. The method is applied to conventional and unconventional reservoir rocks. The results agree with measurements on core plugs. The novel method enables surface relaxivity measurements on rocks where cores are not available.

Title: Spin dynamics: basics of nuclear magnetic resonance
Polymer flooding enhanced oil recovery evaluated with magnetic resonance imaging and relaxation time measurements
Abstract: Polymer flooding is an enhanced oil recovery (EOR) method that improves sweep efficiency by increasing the viscosity of the injected water. This study uses magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and relaxation time measurements to evaluate polymer flooding in core samples. MRI provides spatial maps of oil and water saturation during the flood. Relaxation time measurements provide information on fluid-pore interactions. The results show that polymer flooding recovers more oil than water flooding, especially from heterogeneous regions. The MRI and NMR data provide insights into the mechanisms of polymer flooding at the pore scale.

Title: An NMR-based clay content evaluation method for tight oil reservoirs
Abstract: Clay content is an important parameter for evaluating tight oil reservoirs. This study presents an NMR-based method for evaluating clay content. The method uses the fact that clay-bound water has short T2 relaxation times. The NMR T2 distribution is decomposed into components corresponding to clay-bound water and movable fluids. The amplitude of the short T2 component is correlated with clay content. The method is applied to tight oil reservoir cores and validated with X-ray diffraction data. The NMR method provides a quick and non-destructive way to estimate clay content.

Title: Wettability characterization of low-permeability reservoirs using nuclear magnetic resonance: an experimental study
Abstract: Wettability affects fluid distribution and flow in low-permeability reservoirs. This experimental study uses nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) to characterize wettability in low-permeability cores. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements were performed on cores saturated with brine and oil. The T2 distributions were analyzed to infer wettability. The results show that the T2 spectrum shifts depending on the wetting state. A wettability index is derived from the T2 data and compared with the Amott-Harvey index. The NMR method provides a way to assess wettability in low-permeability rocks where conventional methods are difficult to apply.

Title: Characterization of fluid distributions in porous media by NMR techniques
Abstract: NMR techniques are used to characterize fluid distributions in porous media. The study combines NMR relaxation and diffusion measurements to probe the spatial distribution of fluids. Experiments were performed on model porous media and rock cores with different fluid saturations. The results show that the NMR response is sensitive to the fluid distribution, with different relaxation and diffusion characteristics for different saturation states. The data are analyzed using theoretical models to infer the fluid distribution at the pore scale. The study demonstrates the potential of NMR for non-invasive characterization of multiphase fluid distributions.

Title: NMR state diagram concept
Abstract: The NMR state diagram concept is introduced as a way to characterize the physical states of water and other components in food materials. The diagram is based on NMR relaxation time measurements, which are sensitive to molecular mobility. The state diagram maps the relaxation times as a function of temperature and composition, revealing different states such as free water, bound water, and ice. The concept is applied to various food systems and provides insights into water dynamics and stability. The NMR state diagram has potential applications in food science and technology.

Title: Quantifying the effect of salinity on oilfield water-in-oil emulsion stability
Abstract: Water-in-oil emulsions are common in oilfield operations and can cause problems in production and processing. This study quantifies the effect of salinity on emulsion stability using NMR. Emulsions were prepared with different brine salinities and crude oils. NMR diffusometry was used to measure droplet size distributions. The results show that increasing salinity reduces emulsion stability, leading to larger droplets and faster coalescence. The effect depends on the crude oil composition and the type of salt. The NMR method provides a quantitative way to assess emulsion stability and the impact of brine chemistry.

Title: Principles and applications of well logging, all days
Effect of paramagnetic mineral content and distribution on nuclear magnetic resonance surface relaxivity in organic-rich Niobrara and Haynesville shales
Abstract: Paramagnetic minerals in shales affect NMR surface relaxivity and complicate the interpretation of NMR data. This study investigates the effect of paramagnetic mineral content and distribution on surface relaxivity in organic-rich Niobrara and Haynesville shales. The shales were characterized by NMR, X-ray diffraction, and electron microscopy. The results show that surface relaxivity is correlated with the content of paramagnetic minerals such as pyrite and illite. The distribution of these minerals also matters, with surface relaxivity being higher when they are located on the pore surface. The study provides insights into the controls on NMR relaxation in shales and the implications for pore size estimation.

Title: Wettability index determination from NMR logs
Robust determination of surface relaxivity from nuclear magnetic resonance DT2 measurements
Abstract: Surface relaxivity is a key parameter for converting NMR relaxation time distributions to pore size distributions. This study presents a robust method for determining surface relaxivity from NMR D-T2 measurements. The method uses the correlation between diffusion coefficient (D) and relaxation time (T2) to separate the effects of pore size and surface relaxivity. The D-T2 data are analyzed using a joint inversion to obtain the surface relaxivity. The method is applied to model porous media and rock cores. The results are consistent with independent measurements. The robust method improves the accuracy of pore size estimation from NMR.

Title: Heavy oil characterization: lessons learned during placement of a horizontal injector at a tar/oil interface
Porosity distributions in carbonate reservoirs using low-field NMR
Thermal maturity history and petroleum generation modelling for the Lower Cretaceous Abu Gabra Formation in the Fula Sub-basin, Muglad Basin, Sudan
Abstract: The thermal maturity history and petroleum generation potential of the Lower Cretaceous Abu Gabra Formation in the Fula Sub-basin, Muglad Basin, Sudan, are evaluated using organic geochemistry and basin modeling. The Abu Gabra Formation contains organic-rich shales with good to excellent source rock potential. Thermal maturity varies from immature to early mature in the study area. Basin modeling indicates that petroleum generation started in the Late Cretaceous and continues to the present. The modeling results are consistent with the occurrence of oil and gas shows in the basin. The study provides insights into the petroleum system of the Muglad Basin.

Title: Reservoir quality and its controlling diagenetic factors in the Bentiu Formation, Northeastern Muglad Basin, Sudan
Abstract: The reservoir quality of the Bentiu Formation in the Northeastern Muglad Basin, Sudan, is investigated using petrographic, mineralogical, and petrophysical analyses. The Bentiu Formation consists of sandstones with varying porosity and permeability. The reservoir quality is controlled by diagenetic processes, including compaction, cementation, and dissolution. The main cements are quartz, clay minerals, and carbonates. Dissolution of feldspars and rock fragments has created secondary porosity. The best reservoir quality is found in sandstones with minimal cementation and good sorting. The study provides insights into the factors controlling reservoir quality in the Bentiu Formation.

Title: Impact of EOR Huff-n-puff on rock microstructure
Ultrafast diffusion exchange nuclear magnetic resonance
Abstract: Diffusion exchange NMR is a powerful technique for studying molecular exchange between different environments. However, conventional methods are slow and require long measurement times. This paper presents an ultrafast diffusion exchange NMR method that accelerates the measurement by two orders of magnitude. The method uses spatial encoding of the diffusion dimension and parallel detection. It is demonstrated on model systems and porous media. The ultrafast method enables real-time monitoring of dynamic processes and opens new possibilities for studying exchange in materials science and biology.

Title: In-situ heavy oil viscosity prediction at high temperatures using low-field NMR relaxometry and nonlinear least squares
Abstract: Heavy oil viscosity is a key parameter for production and processing. This study presents a method for predicting in-situ heavy oil viscosity at high temperatures using low-field NMR relaxometry and nonlinear least squares. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements were performed on heavy oil samples at different temperatures. The T2 data were correlated with viscosity using a nonlinear model. The model parameters were optimized using nonlinear least squares. The predicted viscosities agree with rheometer measurements. The NMR method provides a quick and non-destructive way to estimate heavy oil viscosity under reservoir conditions.

Title: Petrophysical measurements on shales using NMR
Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy
Magnetization evolution in connected pore systems
Abstract: The evolution of nuclear magnetization in connected pore systems is studied theoretically. The model accounts for diffusion coupling between pores of different sizes. The magnetization decay is multiexponential and depends on the pore geometry and connectivity. The theory predicts that the relaxation time distribution is affected by pore coupling, with faster relaxation in connected systems. The results are relevant for interpreting NMR data in rocks and other porous media with interconnected pores.

Title: Modified spin-echo method for measuring nuclear relaxation times
Abstract: A modified spin-echo method for measuring nuclear relaxation times is presented. The method uses a sequence of 90° and 180° pulses to refocus the magnetization and eliminate the effects of diffusion and field inhomogeneity. The echo amplitude decays with the transverse relaxation time T2. The method is applied to liquids and solids and provides accurate T2 measurements. The modified spin-echo method is widely used in NMR spectroscopy and imaging.

Title: Nuclear magnetic relaxation in porous media
Determination of wettability from magnetic resonance relaxation and diffusion measurements on fresh state cores
Validation of NMR relaxation exchange time measurements in porous media
Abstract: NMR relaxation exchange measurements provide information on molecular exchange between different environments in porous media. This study validates relaxation exchange time measurements using model systems and numerical simulations. The experiments were performed on porous media with controlled pore sizes and connectivity. The exchange times derived from NMR agree with theoretical predictions. The validation confirms that relaxation exchange NMR is a reliable method for studying pore connectivity and exchange processes in porous media.

Title: Nuclear magnetic resonance relaxation and diffusion in the presence of internal gradients: the effect of magnetic field strength
Abstract: Internal magnetic field gradients in porous media affect NMR relaxation and diffusion measurements. This study investigates the effect of magnetic field strength on internal gradients and their impact on NMR data. Experiments were performed on rock cores at different field strengths. The results show that internal gradients increase with field strength, leading to enhanced relaxation and diffusion attenuation. The effect is more pronounced in rocks with high susceptibility contrast. The study provides guidelines for selecting the appropriate field strength and pulse sequences to mitigate the effects of internal gradients.

Title: Numerical estimation of relaxation and diffusion distributions in two dimensions
Abstract: Two-dimensional NMR correlation methods (e.g., T1-T2, D-T2) provide detailed information on fluid populations in porous media. This paper presents numerical methods for estimating relaxation and diffusion distributions from 2D NMR data. The methods are based on inverse Laplace transforms and regularization. The performance of different algorithms is compared using synthetic and experimental data. The numerical methods enable robust and accurate estimation of 2D distributions, which are essential for interpreting NMR data in complex systems.

Title: Quantitative in situ enhanced oil recovery monitoring using nuclear magnetic resonance
Abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is used to monitor enhanced oil recovery (EOR) processes in situ. The study demonstrates quantitative NMR measurements of oil saturation during water flooding and polymer flooding in core samples. NMR T2 relaxation time distributions provide information on oil distribution and mobilization. The results show that NMR can track changes in oil saturation with high sensitivity and spatial resolution. The method is applied to different EOR processes and provides insights into recovery mechanisms. NMR monitoring has potential applications in laboratory core floods and field pilots.

Title: Magnetic resonance imaging in laboratory petrophysical core analysis
Abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a powerful tool for laboratory petrophysical core analysis. This review covers the principles and applications of MRI in core analysis. Topics include fluid saturation mapping, wettability characterization, and flow visualization. MRI provides non-invasive, 3D images of fluid distributions at the core scale. The review discusses the advantages and limitations of MRI compared to other techniques. Case studies demonstrate the use of MRI in studying drainage, imbibition, and EOR processes. MRI is an essential tool for understanding multiphase flow in porous media.

Title: Low-field permanent magnets for industrial process and quality control
Abstract: Low-field permanent magnets are widely used for industrial process and quality control applications of NMR. This review covers the design and performance of low-field magnets, including Halbach arrays and unilateral magnets. Applications include fluid characterization, porous media analysis, and online monitoring. The advantages of low-field NMR are its low cost, portability, and ease of use. The review discusses the challenges of low-field NMR, such as low sensitivity and the effects of internal gradients. Future developments in magnet technology and pulse sequences are outlined.

Title: Real-time oil-saturation monitoring in rock cores with low-field NMR
Abstract: Real-time monitoring of oil saturation in rock cores during flooding experiments is demonstrated using low-field NMR. The method uses a compact NMR instrument that fits inside a core holder. NMR T2 measurements are acquired continuously during the flood, providing quantitative oil saturation data. The method is applied to water flooding and polymer flooding in sandstone cores. The results show that NMR can capture the dynamics of oil displacement with high temporal resolution. Real-time NMR monitoring enables detailed studies of EOR processes and provides data for model validation.

Title: A finite element approach to forward modeling of nuclear magnetic resonance measurements in coupled pore systems
Abstract: A finite element approach is developed for forward modeling of NMR measurements in coupled pore systems. The model solves the Bloch-Torrey equations for magnetization evolution in complex pore geometries. The approach accounts for diffusion coupling between pores and surface relaxation. The model is validated by comparison with analytical solutions and experimental data. The finite element method enables realistic simulations of NMR responses in rocks and other porous media. The model can be used to interpret NMR data and to optimize pulse sequences.

Title: Contributed Review: Nuclear magnetic resonance core analysis at 0.3 T
Abstract: This contributed review covers nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) core analysis at a field strength of 0.3 T. The review discusses the advantages of 0.3 T systems, including low cost, portability, and reduced internal gradients. Applications include porosity, permeability, and fluid saturation measurements. The review also covers pulse sequences, data analysis, and interpretation. Case studies demonstrate the use of 0.3 T NMR in petrophysical core analysis. The review provides a comprehensive overview of the capabilities and limitations of low-field NMR for core analysis.

Title: Quantitative remaining oil interpretation using magnetic resonance: from the laboratory to the pilot
Magnetic resonance imaging of chemical EOR in core to complement field pilot studies
Monitoring chemical EOR processes
Monitoring chemical EOR processes
Industrial applications of magnetic resonance diffusion and relaxation time measurements
Understanding NMR relaxometry of partially water-saturated rocks
Abstract: NMR relaxometry is used to study partially water-saturated rocks. The aim is to understand the relationship between water saturation, pore structure, and NMR response. Experiments were performed on sandstone and carbonate cores at different saturation levels. The T2 relaxation time distributions were analyzed to identify different water populations (e.g., clay-bound, capillary-bound, and free water). The results show that the T2 distribution changes systematically with saturation. The study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR logs in partially saturated reservoirs.

Title: Identifying a bypassed reservoir in limestone 'A' Sequence With Magnetic Resonance While Drilling
Field testing of a new nuclear magnetic resonance logging-while-drilling tool
Ultra-high field NMR and MRI—the role of magnet technology to increase sensitivity and specificity
Abstract: Ultra-high field (UHF) NMR and MRI at field strengths of 7 Tesla and above offer increased sensitivity and specificity. This review focuses on the role of magnet technology in achieving UHF. Topics include magnet design, field homogeneity, and stability. The review also covers RF coil technology and its impact on signal-to-noise ratio and image quality. UHF enables new applications in biomedical research and clinical diagnosis. The review discusses the challenges of UHF, such as safety and cost, and outlines future developments in magnet technology.

Title: Effect of thermal exposure on oil shale saturation and reservoir properties
Abstract: Thermal exposure during in-situ conversion processes affects oil shale saturation and reservoir properties. This study investigates the effect of thermal exposure on oil shale samples from different formations. The samples were heated to different temperatures, and their properties were characterized by NMR, porosity, and permeability measurements. The results show that heating causes changes in organic matter composition, pore structure, and fluid saturation. The NMR T1-T2 maps reveal the evolution of organic and mineral phases. The study provides insights into the thermal behavior of oil shales and the implications for in-situ recovery.

Title: Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy and imaging of humans
Simultaneous assessment of wettability and water saturation using 2D NMR measurements
Abstract: Wettability and water saturation are key reservoir properties that affect fluid distribution and flow. This study presents a method for simultaneous assessment of wettability and water saturation using 2D NMR measurements (T1-T2 and D-T2). The method is based on the fact that the NMR response of oil and water depends on the wetting state and saturation. The 2D NMR data are analyzed using a joint inversion to obtain wettability and saturation. The method is validated on rock cores with different wettability. The simultaneous assessment improves the accuracy of reservoir characterization.

Title: Diffusion NMR spectroscopy
Abstract: Diffusion NMR spectroscopy is a powerful tool for studying molecular motion in biological systems. This review covers the principles of diffusion NMR, including pulsed field gradient methods and data analysis. Applications include the study of tissue microstructure, water compartmentation, and molecular transport. The review also discusses the combination of diffusion NMR with imaging (diffusion MRI). Diffusion NMR provides unique insights into the structure and dynamics of biological systems at the molecular and cellular levels.

Title: Capillary-pressure curves for low-permeability chalk obtained by nuclear magnetic resonance imaging of core-saturation profiles
Abstract: Capillary-pressure curves for low-permeability chalk are obtained by nuclear magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of core-saturation profiles. The method uses a centrifuge to desaturate the core and MRI to measure the saturation profile along the core. The capillary pressure is derived from the saturation profile using the centrifuge data. The method is applied to chalk cores with different permeabilities. The results are compared with conventional mercury injection capillary pressure data. The MRI method provides a non-destructive way to measure capillary pressure in low-permeability rocks.

Title: Thin sand development made possible through enhanced geosteering and reservoir planning with while-drilling resistivity and NMR logs: example from Niger delta
Rock core analysis: metallic core holders for magnetic resonance imaging under reservoir conditions
13C solid-state NMR analysis of the chemical structure in petroleum coke during idealized in situ combustion conditions
Abstract: 13C solid-state NMR is used to analyze the chemical structure of petroleum coke during idealized in situ combustion conditions. Petroleum coke samples were heated to different temperatures in an oxidizing atmosphere, and the chemical changes were monitored by NMR. The results show that the carbon structure evolves from highly aromatic to more graphitic with increasing temperature. Oxygen-containing functional groups are formed at intermediate temperatures. The NMR data provide insights into the combustion kinetics and mechanisms of petroleum coke. The study has implications for in-situ combustion processes in petroleum reservoirs.

Title: Combined high-resolution solid-state 1 H/ 13 C NMR spectroscopy and 1 H NMR relaxometry for the characterization of kerogen thermal maturation
Abstract: Kerogen thermal maturation is studied using combined high-resolution solid-state 1H/13C NMR spectroscopy and 1H NMR relaxometry.
