The methods provide complementary information on the chemical structure and molecular dynamics of kerogen. Solid-state NMR reveals the evolution of functional groups and carbon骨架 with maturation. Relaxometry probes the mobility of organic components. The combined approach is applied to kerogen samples with different thermal maturities. The results show that maturation leads to aromatization, condensation, and reduced mobility. The study provides insights into the thermal transformation of kerogen and its impact on shale properties.

Title: Improved description of organic matter in shales by enhanced solid fraction detection with low-field 1 H NMR relaxometry
From oil-prone source rock to gas-producing shale reservoir – geologic and petrophysical characterization of unconventional shale-gas reservoirs
Chemically induced destabilization of water-in-crude oil emulsions
Abstract: The chemically induced destabilization of water-in-crude oil emulsions is investigated. The study focuses on the effects of demulsifiers on emulsion stability. Experiments were performed on model emulsions and crude oil emulsions. The demulsifier action is characterized by interfacial tension measurements, droplet size analysis, and coalescence studies. The results show that demulsifiers reduce the interfacial film strength and promote coalescence. The efficiency of demulsifiers depends on their chemical structure and concentration. The study provides insights into the mechanisms of emulsion destabilization and the selection of demulsifiers for oilfield applications.

Title: Estimating irreducible water saturation and permeability of sandstones from nuclear magnetic resonance measurements by fractal analysis
Abstract: A method for estimating irreducible water saturation and permeability of sandstones from nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) measurements is presented. The method uses fractal analysis of the NMR T2 distribution. The fractal dimension is derived from the T2 spectrum and correlated with irreducible water saturation and permeability. The method is applied to sandstone cores with different pore structures. The estimated values agree with experimental measurements. The fractal analysis provides a way to extract petrophysical parameters from NMR data without empirical correlations.

Title: Dolomite cores evaluated by NMR
Abstract: Dolomite cores are evaluated by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) to characterize pore structure and fluid properties. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements were performed on dolomite cores with different porosities and permeabilities. The T2 distributions were analyzed to obtain pore size distributions. The NMR results are compared with mercury injection capillary pressure and thin-section analysis. The study shows that NMR provides reliable pore size information for dolomites. The T2 distribution is sensitive to the presence of vugs and fractures. NMR is a valuable tool for characterizing dolomite reservoirs.

Title: The magnetic resonance while-drilling tool: theory and operation
Field test of an experimental Nmr Lwd Device
A new direction in wireline and lwd Nmr
Pulsed-field gradient nuclear magnetic resonance as a tool for studying translational diffusion: Part 1. Basic Theory
NMR studies of translational motion
Multi-scale characterization of unconventional tight carbonate reservoir: insights from October oil filed, Gulf of Suez rift basin, Egypt
Abstract: The October oil field in the Gulf of Suez rift basin, Egypt, contains unconventional tight carbonate reservoirs. This study presents a multi-scale characterization of these reservoirs using petrographic, mineralogical, and petrophysical analyses. The reservoir rocks are mainly limestones and dolomites with low porosity and permeability. The pore structure is complex, with microporosity, fractures, and vugs. The study integrates data from core analysis, well logs, and seismic to understand the reservoir quality and heterogeneity. The results provide insights into the factors controlling reservoir performance and the potential for hydrocarbon production.

Title: Using nmr t2 to predict the drainage capillary curves pc-sw in carbonates reservoirs
Tight gas sands permeability estimation from mercury injection capillary pressure and nuclear magnetic resonance data
Abstract: Permeability estimation in tight gas sands is challenging due to the complex pore structure. This study presents a method for estimating permeability from mercury injection capillary pressure (MICP) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) data. The method uses the pore throat size distribution from MICP and the pore body size distribution from NMR to derive a permeability estimate. The method is applied to tight gas sand cores and validated with measured permeability. The estimated permeabilities agree with the measured values. The combined MICP-NMR method provides a way to estimate permeability in tight gas sands where conventional methods are unreliable.

Title: Integrated petrophysics and geosteering reservoir characterization in the initial development phase of a carbonate reservoir - Campos Basin, Offshore Brazil
The nature of C-S-H in hardened cements
Abstract: The nature of calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) in hardened cements is reviewed. C-S-H is the main binding phase in cement and controls its properties. The review covers the structure, composition, and morphology of C-S-H. Different models of C-S-H are discussed, including the tobermorite-based model and the colloidal model. The review also covers the effects of aging, temperature, and additives on C-S-H. The understanding of C-S-H is essential for improving cement performance and durability.

Title: NMR permeability estimators in 'chalk' carbonate rocks obtained under different relaxation times and MICP size scalings
Abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) permeability estimators are evaluated for 'chalk' carbonate rocks. The study compares different NMR permeability models based on relaxation time distributions and pore size scaling from mercury injection capillary pressure (MICP). The results show that the accuracy of NMR permeability estimates depends on the choice of relaxation time threshold and the scaling factor. The study provides recommendations for selecting appropriate NMR permeability estimators for chalk reservoirs.

Title: NMR studies of an iron-rich sandstone oil reservoir
Enhanced oil recovery by spontaneous imbibition of imidazolium based ionic liquids on the carbonate reservoir
Benchmarking of pulsed field gradient nuclear magnetic resonance as a demulsifier selection tool with arabian light crude oils
A nuclear magnetic method for determining the permeability of sandstones
Nuclear magnetic resonance logging: while drilling, wireline, and fluid sampling
Geosteering and mapping of complex reservoir boundaries using an integrated data approach
Reservoir characterization while drilling; a real time geosteering answer to maximize well values. A case study, Offshore Abu Dhabi
Relaxation and relaxation exchange NMR to characterise asphaltene adsorption and wettability dynamics in siliceous systems
Abstract: Asphaltene adsorption and wettability dynamics in siliceous systems are studied using relaxation and relaxation exchange NMR. The methods provide information on the interactions between asphaltenes and the silica surface and their effect on wettability. The results show that asphaltene adsorption alters the surface relaxivity and the wetting state. Relaxation exchange NMR reveals the exchange of molecules between different environments. The study provides insights into the mechanisms of wettability alteration by asphaltenes and the potential for NMR to monitor these processes.

Title: On the optimum aging time: magnetic resonance study of asphaltene adsorption dynamics in sandstone rock
Abstract: The dynamics of asphaltene adsorption in sandstone rock are studied by magnetic resonance to determine the optimum aging time for wettability alteration. NMR relaxation time measurements were performed on sandstone cores aged with asphaltene solutions for different times. The results show that asphaltene adsorption changes the surface relaxivity and the T2 distribution. The adsorption kinetics follow a Langmuir-type behavior, with equilibrium reached after a certain aging time. The optimum aging time for wettability alteration is determined from the NMR data. The study provides insights into the time scale of asphaltene adsorption and its impact on reservoir wettability.

Title: Organic matter detection in shale reservoirs using a novel pulse sequence for T1–T2 relaxation maps at 2 MHz
Abstract: A novel pulse sequence for acquiring T1-T2 relaxation maps at 2 MHz is presented for organic matter detection in shale reservoirs. The sequence combines saturation recovery and CPMG to measure T1 and T2 simultaneously. The method is applied to shale samples and successfully distinguishes organic matter from pore fluids based on their T1-T2 signatures. The T1/T2 ratio is sensitive to the organic matter type and maturity. The novel pulse sequence improves the speed and resolution of T1-T2 measurements and has potential applications in shale evaluation.

Title: 1D and 2D NMR core-log integration in organic shale
The Role of asphaltenes in stabilizing water-in-crude oil emulsions
The role of asphaltenes in stabilizing water-in-crude oil emulsions
Application of nuclear magnetic resonance permeability models in tight reservoirs
Abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) permeability models are evaluated for tight reservoirs. The study compares different NMR permeability models, including the Schlumberger-Doll Research (SDR) model and the Timur-Coates model, using data from tight sandstone and carbonate cores. The results show that the accuracy of the models depends on the pore structure and the presence of microporosity. Modifications to the models are proposed to improve permeability estimation in tight reservoirs. The study provides guidance for selecting and calibrating NMR permeability models for tight reservoirs.

Title: Microstructural studies of gas shales
Novel NMR techniques for porous media research
Abstract: Novel NMR techniques for porous media research are reviewed. The techniques include two-dimensional correlation methods (T1-T2, D-T2), relaxation exchange spectroscopy, and diffusion-diffusion exchange spectroscopy. These methods provide detailed information on pore structure, fluid populations, and molecular exchange. Applications to rocks, cements, and other porous materials are discussed. The novel techniques expand the capabilities of NMR for characterizing complex porous media and understanding transport processes.

Title: NMR application in unconventional shale reservoirs – a new porous media research frontier
Abstract: NMR applications in unconventional shale reservoirs are reviewed, highlighting the challenges and opportunities. Shales have complex pore structures and organic matter that affect NMR responses. The review covers NMR relaxometry, diffusometry, and imaging for characterizing shale properties such as porosity, pore size distribution, wettability, and fluid saturation. Novel methods, including T1-T2 maps and high-field NMR, are discussed. The review identifies future research directions for NMR in shales, including the integration of NMR with other techniques and the development of new pulse sequences.

Title: Experimental identification of diffusive coupling using 2D NMR
Abstract: Diffusive coupling between pores in porous media is experimentally identified using two-dimensional NMR (T2-T2 exchange). The experiments were performed on model porous media with controlled pore sizes and connectivity. The 2D NMR spectra show cross-peaks that indicate exchange between pores. The cross-peak intensity depends on the coupling strength and the experimental parameters. The results are compared with theoretical predictions. The study demonstrates that 2D NMR can be used to detect and quantify diffusive coupling in porous media.

Title: Spin diffusion measurements: spin echoes in the presence of a time-dependent field gradient
Abstract: A method for measuring spin diffusion using spin echoes in the presence of a time-dependent field gradient is presented. The method uses a pulsed field gradient to encode spatial information and measure the diffusion coefficient. The theory of the experiment is developed, and experimental results on water and glycerol are presented. The pulsed field gradient method is widely used for diffusion measurements in NMR and MRI.

Title: Application of MRI in the measurement of two-phase flow of supercritical CO2 and water in porous rocks
Abstract: MRI is used to measure two-phase flow of supercritical CO2 and water in porous rocks. The experiments simulate CO2 sequestration in saline aquifers. MRI provides images of the fluid distribution during drainage and imbibition. The results show the displacement patterns and the residual trapping of CO2. The study demonstrates the potential of MRI for studying multiphase flow in porous media under reservoir conditions.

Title: A modular and multi-functional single-sided NMR sensor
Abstract: A modular and multi-functional single-sided NMR sensor is designed and tested. The sensor uses a permanent magnet array to produce a homogeneous field outside the sensor. The sensor can be configured for different applications, including relaxometry, diffusometry, and imaging. The performance is demonstrated on rock cores and other materials. The modular design allows for easy customization and optimization. The sensor has potential applications in well logging, materials testing, and process control.

Title: Design of a new LWD NMR tool with high mechanical reliability
Abstract: A new logging-while-drilling (LWD) NMR tool with high mechanical reliability is designed. The tool uses a robust magnet and antenna assembly to withstand the harsh drilling environment. The design is optimized for high signal-to-noise ratio and fast logging speed. The tool is tested in laboratory and field conditions, demonstrating reliable performance. The new LWD NMR tool enables real-time formation evaluation while drilling.

Title: Study on nuclear magnetic resonance logging T2 spectrum shape correction of sandstone reservoirs in oil-based mud wells
Abstract: Oil-based mud (OBM) filtrate invasion affects NMR logging responses in sandstone reservoirs. This study presents a method for correcting the T2 spectrum shape for OBM effects. The method uses a combination of laboratory measurements on core samples and numerical simulations. The correction accounts for the different relaxation characteristics of OBM and formation fluids. The corrected T2 spectra provide more accurate pore size distributions and fluid saturations. The method is applied to field data and validated with production tests.

Title: Primary drainage capillary pressure curves in heterogeneous carbonates with ultracentrifuge and NMR
NMR petrophysical interpretation method of gas shale based on core NMR experiment
Abstract: An NMR petrophysical interpretation method for gas shale is developed based on core NMR experiments. The method uses T2 relaxation time distributions to estimate porosity, pore size distribution, and fluid saturation. The effects of organic matter and clay minerals on NMR responses are considered. The method is applied to gas shale cores and validated with independent measurements. The NMR-derived petrophysical parameters agree with conventional core analysis. The method provides a basis for interpreting NMR logs in gas shale reservoirs.

Title: Influential factors analysis and porosity correction method of nuclear magnetic resonance measurement in igneous rocks
Abstract: The influential factors on NMR measurements in igneous rocks are analyzed, and a porosity correction method is developed. The factors include paramagnetic minerals, internal gradients, and pore structure. NMR experiments were performed on igneous rock cores with different compositions. The results show that paramagnetic minerals reduce the NMR porosity and T2 relaxation times. A correction method based on the mineralogy and magnetic susceptibility is proposed. The corrected NMR porosity agrees with helium porosity. The study improves the accuracy of NMR porosity in igneous rocks.

Title: Effect of internal magnetic-field gradients on nuclear-magnetic-resonance measurements and nuclear-magnetic-resonance-based pore-network characterization
Abstract: The effect of internal magnetic-field gradients on NMR measurements and pore-network characterization is investigated. Internal gradients arise from susceptibility differences between the solid matrix and pore fluids. They affect relaxation times and diffusion measurements. The study uses numerical simulations and experiments on rock cores to quantify the effects. The results show that internal gradients can lead to errors in pore size estimation and permeability prediction. A method for correcting NMR data for internal gradients is proposed. The study improves the accuracy of NMR-based pore-network characterization.

Title: Wettability quantification in mixed-wet rocks using a new NMR-based method
A new NMR-based method for wettability assessment in mixed-wet rocks
Real- time detection of tar in carbonates using LWD triple combo
Probing diffusion dynamics during hydrate formation by high field NMR relaxometry and diffusometry
Abstract: Diffusion dynamics during hydrate formation are probed by high-field NMR relaxometry and diffusometry. The experiments monitor the formation of methane hydrate in porous media. NMR relaxation times and diffusion coefficients change as hydrate forms and consumes water and gas. The results provide insights into the kinetics of hydrate formation and the distribution of phases. The study demonstrates the potential of NMR for studying gas hydrates in natural and engineered systems.

Title: Solutions of ill-posed problems
Pulsed nuclear magnetic resonance studies of porosity, movable fluid, and permeability of sandstones
Abstract: Pulsed nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is used to study porosity, movable fluid, and permeability of sandstones. The NMR measurements provide relaxation time distributions that are correlated with pore size and fluid saturation. The movable fluid fraction is estimated from the NMR signal. Permeability is estimated from the relaxation times using empirical correlations. The study establishes the basis for using NMR in petrophysical evaluation of sandstones.

Title: Nuclear-magnetic-resonance response of brine, oil, and methane in organic-rich shales
Abstract: The NMR response of brine, oil, and methane in organic-rich shales is investigated. The study aims to understand the factors affecting NMR relaxation in shales and to develop methods for fluid typing. NMR measurements were performed on shale cores saturated with different fluids. The results show that the relaxation times are affected by organic matter, clay minerals, and pore size. The T1-T2 maps can distinguish between brine, oil, and methane based on their relaxation characteristics. The study provides insights for interpreting NMR logs in shale reservoirs.

Title: Analysis of NMR diffusion coupling effects in two-phase carbonate rocks: comparison of measurements with Monte Carlo simulations
Adsorption layers of water on the surface of hydrophilic, hydrophobic and mixed silicas
Abstract: Adsorption layers of water on the surface of hydrophilic, hydrophobic, and mixed silicas are studied by 1H NMR spectroscopy. The NMR signal from adsorbed water is used to probe the structure and dynamics of the adsorption layers. The results show that the thickness and mobility of the water layer depend on the surface chemistry. On hydrophilic surfaces, water forms multilayer adsorption with high mobility. On hydrophobic surfaces, water forms clusters with reduced mobility. Mixed surfaces show intermediate behavior. The study provides insights into water-solid interactions at the molecular level.

Title: Study of hydrated structures on the surface of mesoporous silicas and carbosils by 1 H NMR spectroscopy of adsorbed water
Abstract: Hydrated structures on the surface of mesoporous silicas and carbosils are studied by 1H NMR spectroscopy of adsorbed water. The NMR signal is used to characterize the state of water in the pores. The results show that water exists in different states, including bound water and free water, depending on the pore size and surface chemistry. The fraction of bound water increases with decreasing pore size and increasing surface hydrophilicity. The study provides insights into the hydration of porous materials and the factors controlling water behavior.

Title: Laboratory and downhole wettability from NMR T1/T2 ratio
Laboratory and downhole wettability from NMR T1/T2 ratio
A review of 60 years of NMR wettability
Abstract: This review covers 60 years of NMR wettability research. The review traces the development of NMR methods for wettability assessment, from early relaxation time measurements to modern 2D correlation methods. The physical basis of NMR wettability is explained, including the effects of surface interactions and fluid distributions. Applications to reservoir rocks and other porous media are discussed. The review identifies key milestones and future directions in NMR wettability research.

Title: Downhole wettability: the potential of NMR
Solving Fredholm integrals of the first kind with tensor product structure in 2 and 2.5 dimensions
Abstract: A method for solving Fredholm integrals of the first kind with tensor product structure in 2 and 2.5 dimensions is presented. The method is used for inverting NMR relaxation and diffusion data to obtain 2D distributions (e.g., T1-T2, D-T2). The algorithm uses a combination of regularization and compression to achieve fast and stable inversion. The method is demonstrated on synthetic and experimental data. It is widely used in NMR data analysis.

Title: Experimental study of the effects of wettability and fluid saturation on nuclear magnetic resonance and dielectric measurements in limestone
LWD as the absolute formation evaluation technology: present-day capabilities, limitations, and future developments of LWD technology
Measurement of spin relaxation in complex systems
Abstract: A method for measuring spin relaxation in complex systems is presented. The method uses a pulse sequence that separates the effects of different relaxation mechanisms. It is applied to liquids and solids to measure T1 and T2. The method provides insights into molecular dynamics and interactions. It is widely used in NMR spectroscopy.

Title: When is the optimal regularization parameter insensitive to the choice of the loss function?
Principles of emulsion formation
Abstract: The principles of emulsion formation are reviewed. The review covers the thermodynamics and kinetics of emulsification, including the roles of surfactants, energy input, and flow conditions. The mechanisms of droplet breakup and coalescence are discussed. The review also covers the properties of emulsions, such as droplet size distribution and stability. The principles are essential for understanding and controlling emulsion formation in various applications.

Title: Low-field nuclear magnetic resonance characterization of carbonate and sandstone reservoirs from rock spring uplift of wyoming
Abstract: Low-field NMR is used to characterize carbonate and sandstone reservoirs from the Rock Spring Uplift of Wyoming. NMR T2 relaxation time measurements provide information on pore size distributions and fluid saturations. The results are compared with conventional core analysis. The study shows that NMR can distinguish between different pore types and fluid populations. The NMR data are used to estimate permeability and to evaluate reservoir quality. The study demonstrates the value of low-field NMR for reservoir characterization.

Title: Theoretical investigation of heterogeneous wettability in porous media using NMR
Abstract: Heterogeneous wettability in porous media is investigated theoretically using NMR. The study develops a model that relates the NMR response to the distribution of wetting and non-wetting phases. The model accounts for the effects of surface relaxivity and diffusion coupling. Simulations are performed on pore networks with different wettability patterns. The results show that the NMR T2 distribution is sensitive to the wettability heterogeneity. The study provides insights into the interpretation of NMR data in mixed-wet rocks.

Title: Effect of CO2-brine-rock reactions on pore architecture and permeability in dolostone: implications for CO2 storage and EOR
Abstract: The effect of CO2-brine-rock reactions on pore architecture and permeability in dolostone is investigated. The experiments simulate CO2 storage and enhanced oil recovery (EOR) conditions. Dolostone cores were reacted with CO2-saturated brine at reservoir conditions. NMR and other techniques were used to characterize the changes in pore structure and permeability. The results show that mineral dissolution and precipitation alter the pore architecture and affect permeability. The study provides insights into the geochemical reactions during CO2 injection and their impact on reservoir properties.

Title: Using NMR to test molecular mobility during a chemical reaction
Abstract: NMR is used to test molecular mobility during a chemical reaction. The study monitors the changes in relaxation times and diffusion coefficients as the reaction proceeds. The results show that molecular mobility changes with the reaction progress, reflecting changes in molecular size and interactions. The NMR method provides insights into the dynamics of reacting systems and has potential applications in studying reaction mechanisms and kinetics.

Title: Estimating capillary pressure from NMR measurements using a pore-size-dependent fluid substitution method
Relaxation mechanisms and shales
Abstract: The relaxation mechanisms in shales are reviewed. Shales have complex compositions, including organic matter, clay minerals, and paramagnetic minerals, which affect NMR relaxation. The review covers the contributions of different relaxation mechanisms, including surface relaxation, bulk relaxation, and diffusion in internal gradients. The effects of pore size, fluid type, and saturation are discussed. The review provides a framework for interpreting NMR data in shales and identifying the key factors controlling relaxation.

Title: Tracking pore to pore exchange using relaxation exchange spectroscopy
Abstract: Relaxation exchange spectroscopy is used to track pore-to-pore exchange in porous media. The method measures the exchange of molecules between different pore environments based on their relaxation times. Experiments were performed on model porous media and rock cores. The results show cross-peaks in the 2D relaxation exchange spectra that indicate exchange between pores. The exchange rate depends on the pore connectivity and the diffusion coefficient. The method provides a way to study pore connectivity and mass transport in porous media.

Title: Simultaneous Gaussian and exponential inversion for improved analysis of shales by NMR relaxometry
Abstract: A simultaneous Gaussian and exponential inversion method is developed for improved analysis of shales by NMR relaxometry. The method fits the NMR relaxation data with a combination of Gaussian and exponential components to account for the different relaxation behaviors of organic matter and pore fluids. The method is applied to shale cores and provides better separation of the organic and fluid signals. The improved analysis enhances the accuracy of porosity and fluid saturation estimates in shales.

Title: Characterizing porous media with NMR methods
Comparing strengths of surface interactions for reactants and solvents in porous catalysts using two-dimensional NMR relaxation correlations
Abstract: Two-dimensional NMR relaxation correlations are used to compare the strengths of surface interactions for reactants and solvents in porous catalysts. The T1-T2 correlation maps provide information on the dynamics of molecules near the catalyst surface. The results show that reactants have stronger surface interactions than solvents, as indicated by shorter relaxation times. The strength of interaction depends on the chemical nature of the molecules and the catalyst surface. The study demonstrates the potential of 2D NMR for studying adsorption and catalysis in porous materials.

Title: Electron paramagnetic resonance, contemporary physics
NMR as a powerful tool to study lithium ion battery electrolytes
Fundamentals of diffusion measurements using NMR
Statistical approach to the solution of first-kind integral equations arising in the study of materials and their properties
Review of NMR spectroscopy: basic principles, concepts and applications in chemistry
Abstract: This review covers the basic principles, concepts, and applications of NMR spectroscopy in chemistry. Topics include nuclear spin, chemical shift, coupling constants, and relaxation. The review also covers 1D and 2D NMR techniques and their applications in structure elucidation and dynamics studies. The review is aimed at students and researchers new to NMR.

Title: Crude oil emulsion: a review on formation, classification and stability of water-in-oil emulsions
Abstract: This review covers the formation, classification, and stability of water-in-oil emulsions in the petroleum industry. The factors affecting emulsion stability, including crude oil composition, water content, and process conditions, are discussed. The review also covers the methods for characterizing and destabilizing emulsions. The understanding of emulsion behavior is essential for optimizing production and processing operations.

Title: Clay minerals in sandstones: controls on formation, distribution and evolution
Combining large-sized model flow experiment and NMR measurement to investigate drilling induced formation damage in sandstone reservoir
Abstract: Drilling-induced formation damage in sandstone reservoirs is investigated by combining large-sized model flow experiments and NMR measurements. The experiments simulate mud filtrate invasion and its effect on permeability. NMR T2 measurements provide information on the changes in pore structure and fluid distribution. The results show that mud filtrate invasion causes fines migration and pore blocking, reducing permeability. The NMR data are used to quantify the damage and to evaluate the effectiveness of damage mitigation strategies.

Title: A new method for predicting capillary pressure curves based on NMR echo data: Sandstone as an example
Abstract: A new method for predicting capillary pressure curves based on NMR echo data is presented, using sandstone as an example. The method uses the full NMR echo train, rather than just the T2 distribution, to capture more information about pore structure. Machine learning is used to relate the echo data to capillary pressure curves from mercury injection. The method is applied to sandstone cores and shows good prediction accuracy. The new method enables capillary pressure estimation from NMR logs without the need for T2 inversion.

Title: A new methodology of constructing pseudo capillary pressure (Pc) curves from nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) logs
Abstract: A new methodology for constructing pseudo capillary pressure (Pc) curves from nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) logs is presented. The method uses the relationship between NMR T2 distributions and pore throat sizes derived from mercury injection capillary pressure (MICP) data. A transform function is developed to convert T2 distributions to pseudo Pc curves. The method is applied to sandstone and carbonate reservoirs and validated with core data. The pseudo Pc curves provide continuous capillary pressure information along the wellbore, which is useful for reservoir characterization and modeling.

Title: The influence factors of NMR logging porosity in complex fluid reservoir
A robust NMR method to measure porosity of low porosity rocks
Abstract: A robust NMR method for measuring porosity of low porosity rocks is presented. The method addresses the challenges of low signal-to-noise ratio and the effects of internal gradients. It uses a optimized pulse sequence and data processing to improve the accuracy of porosity measurements. The method is applied to tight sandstone and carbonate cores with porosities below 5%. The NMR porosities agree with helium porosimetry. The robust method enables reliable porosity evaluation in low porosity reservoirs.

Title: 23 Na and 1 H NMR relaxometry of shale at high magnetic field
Abstract: 23Na and 1H NMR relaxometry of shale at high magnetic field is used to study the interactions between sodium ions and the shale matrix. The 23Na relaxation times are sensitive to the local environment of sodium ions, including their association with clay minerals and organic matter. The 1H relaxation times provide information on the organic matter and pore fluids. The combined 23Na and 1H NMR data provide insights into the chemical and physical properties of shales. The study demonstrates the potential of multinuclear NMR for shale characterization.

Title: Quantitative tortuosity measurements of carbonate rocks using pulsed field gradient NMR
Abstract: Quantitative tortuosity measurements of carbonate rocks are performed using pulsed field gradient (PFG) NMR. Tortuosity is a key parameter for understanding transport properties in porous media. The PFG NMR method measures the restricted diffusion of fluids in the pore space, from which the tortuosity is derived. The method is applied to carbonate cores with different pore structures. The results show that tortuosity varies with pore type and connectivity. The PFG NMR tortuosity measurements are compared with electrical resistivity measurements and show good agreement. The study provides a non-destructive method for tortuosity characterization in carbonates.

Title: Shale rock core analysis using NMR: effect of bitumen and water content
Abstract: The effect of bitumen and water content on NMR measurements in shale cores is investigated. Shale samples with different bitumen and water saturations were analyzed using NMR relaxometry. The results show that bitumen has short relaxation times and can overlap with the signal from clay-bound water. The T1-T2 maps help to distinguish between bitumen and water based on their relaxation characteristics. The study provides guidelines for interpreting NMR data in shales with significant bitumen content and for quantifying fluid saturations.

Title: Characterization of porous media by T2–T2 correlation beyond fast diffusion limit
Abstract: T2-T2 correlation NMR is used to characterize porous media beyond the fast diffusion limit. The method probes the exchange of molecules between different pore environments. The study develops a theoretical framework for interpreting T2-T2 exchange spectra in systems where diffusion is not fast enough to average the relaxation within pores. Simulations and experiments on model porous media demonstrate the sensitivity of T2-T2 exchange to pore size, connectivity, and diffusion coefficient. The method extends the applicability of T2-T2 correlation to a wider range of porous materials.

Title: A novel process for demulsification of water-in-crude oil emulsions by dense carbon dioxide
Abstract: A novel process for demulsification of water-in-crude oil emulsions using dense carbon dioxide is presented. The process exploits the solubility of CO2 in oil and its effect on the interfacial properties. Experiments were performed on model and crude oil emulsions at different CO2 pressures. The results show that dense CO2 promotes coalescence of water droplets and separates the water phase. The demulsification efficiency depends on the CO2 pressure, temperature, and crude oil composition. The process offers a non-toxic and efficient alternative to chemical demulsifiers.

Title: Interpretation of wettability in sandstones with NMR analysis
Effect of organic-matter type and thermal maturity on methane adsorption in shale-gas systems
Abstract: The effect of organic-matter type and thermal maturity on methane adsorption in shale-gas systems is investigated. Methane adsorption isotherms were measured on shale samples with different kerogen types and maturities. The results show that adsorption capacity depends on the organic matter content, type, and maturity. Type II kerogen has higher adsorption capacity than Type III at similar maturity. Adsorption capacity increases with thermal maturity up to a point, then decreases. The study provides insights into the factors controlling gas storage in shales and has implications for resource assessment.

Title: Direct correlation of diffusion and pore size distributions with low field NMR
Abstract: A method for direct correlation of diffusion and pore size distributions using low-field NMR is presented. The method uses pulsed field gradient (PFG) NMR to measure the diffusion coefficient and T2 relaxation simultaneously. The D-T2 correlation maps provide information on the relationship between pore size and molecular mobility. The method is applied to model porous media and rock cores. The results show that the D-T2 correlation can distinguish between different fluid populations and pore environments. The direct correlation improves the characterization of pore structure and fluid dynamics.

Title: Diffusion in internal field gradients
Nuclear magnetic resonance surface relaxivity and its advanced application in calculating pore size distributions
CO2 flooding enhanced oil recovery evaluated using magnetic resonance imaging technique
Abstract: CO2 flooding enhanced oil recovery (EOR) is evaluated using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The experiments simulate CO2 injection in sandstone cores under reservoir conditions. MRI provides images of oil saturation during the flood, showing the displacement patterns and the remaining oil distribution. The results show that CO2 flooding recovers significant oil, but some oil remains trapped in smaller pores. The MRI data are used to quantify the recovery efficiency and to identify the mechanisms of oil mobilization. The study demonstrates the value of MRI for EOR research.

Title: A review of nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) technology applied in the characterization of polymer gels for petroleum reservoir conformance control
Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy for medical and dental applications: a comprehensive review
Abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy is one of the most significant analytical techniques that has been developed in the past few decades. A broad range of biological and nonbiological applications ranging from an individual cell to organs and tissues has been investigated through NMR. Various aspects of this technique are still under research, and many functions of the NMR are still pending a better understanding and acknowledgment. Therefore, this review is aimed at providing a general overview of the main principles, types of this technique, and the advantages and disadvantages of NMR spectroscopy. In addition, an insight into the current uses of NMR in the field of medicine and dentistry and ongoing developments of NMR spectroscopy for future applications has been discussed.
